content: {"answer":"From a U.S. regulatory standpoint under DSHEA, the dividing line between lawful intermittent-fasting guidance and an unlawful disease claim turns on the type of claim made on meal-plan apps and nutraceutical labels. Under section 403(r)(6), a structure/function claim may only describe the role of a nutrient or dietary ingredient in affecting the normal structure or function of the human body [Source 1, Source 4]. DSHEA amended the FD&C Act to establish this framework and distinguish dietary supplements from drugs, which previously had to undergo drug approval if they bore health-related claims [Source 7, Source 9]. Whether a given statement is a permissible structure/function claim or an impermissible disease claim is a fact-specific inquiry guided by FDA's Small Entity Compliance Guide and related factors [Source 2, Source 5]. Marketers must also bear the mandatory DSHEA disclaimer stating the product has not been evaluated by FDA and is not intended to diagnose, treat, cure, or prevent disease, though FDA has recently signaled flexibility on placement [Source 8, Source 6]. For claims asserting a relationship to a disease or health-related condition, FDA recognizes authorized health claims meeting the 'significant scientific agreement' (SSA) standard among qualified experts based on the totality of publicly available evidence [Source 11, Source 14], and qualified health claims supported by credible but lesser evidence [Source 12, Source 13]. A federal court has held that applying SSA preapproval to certain health claims failed intermediate scrutiny [Source 15]. The search results do not address Codex Alimentarius labeling standards, specific evidentiary thresholds for intermittent fasting, or vulnerable-population warning requirements; that information is missing and cannot be answered from these sources.","key_findings":["DSHEA section 403(r)(6) permits structure/function claims describing effects on normal body structure/function, distinguishing supplements from drugs [Source 1, Source 4, Source 7]","The structure/function vs. disease claim determination is fact-specific, governed by FDA guidance documents and multi-factor analysis [Source 2, Source 5]","The mandatory DSHEA disclaimer (not evaluated by FDA; not intended to diagnose/treat/cure/prevent disease) remains required, with recent FDA flexibility on placement [Source 6, Source 8]","Disease-related health claims require either 'significant scientific agreement' (authorized) or credible evidence (qualified), assessed against the totality of publicly available science [Source 11, Source 12, Source 14]","Search results contain no Codex Alimentarius material, no intermittent-fasting-specific thresholds, and no vulnerable-population warning rules\u2014these aspects cannot be answered"],"sources_used":["https://www.fda.gov/food/nutrition-food-labeling-and-critical-foods/structurefunction-claims","https://www.fda.gov/regulatory-information/search-fda-guidance-documents/small-entity-compliance-guide-structurefunction-claims","https://www.fda.gov/food/information-industry-dietary-supplements/notifications-structurefunction-and-related-claims-dietary-supplement-labeling","https://www.venable.com/files/upload/FDLI-Dietary_Supplements.pdf","https://www.chpa.org/news/2021/11/understanding-dietary-supplement-claims","https://dash.harvard.edu/bitstreams/7312037c-ae2f-6bd4-e053-0100007fdf3b/download","https://www.healthlawadvisor.com/fda-signals-flexibility-on-placement-of-the-dshea-disclaimer","https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/FR-2000-01-06/pdf/00-53.pdf","https://www.fda.gov/food/nutrition-food-labeling-and-critical-foods/authorized-health-claims-meet-significant-scientific-agreement-ssa-standard","https://www.fda.gov/food/nutrition-food-labeling-and-critical-foods/qualified-health-claims","https://foodmedcenter.org/claims-matter-health-claims-significant-scientific-agreement-and-qualified-health-clai/","https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-21/chapter-I/subchapter-B/part-101/subpart-E","https://www.congress.gov/crs_external_products/R/PDF/R48623/R48623.2.pdf"]}
